comments.content: This excerpt windowing does byte arithmetic (`modelIndex - before`, `modelEnd + after`, and the subsequent adjustments) on `content`, which can be multibyte UTF-8 — the boundary set explicitly handles CJK punctuation like '。'/'！', and the source content is fetched from an external Codex notice URL. The computed `start`/`end` byte offsets can land in the middle of a rune, producing an excerpt with corrupted/invalid UTF-8. Since this excerpt flows into `LastError` and downstream AI/alert payloads, invalid UTF-8 can break JSON encoding or display. Snap `start`/`end` to rune boundaries before slicing (e.g. using `utf8.RuneStart`).

The range loop reuses the name `value`, shadowing the `value any` function parameter. It works today only because the loop variable's scope is confined to the loop body, so the later `value.(string)` still targets the parameter. This is easy to misread and a future edit (e.g., moving the type assertion or wrapping it) could silently change semantics. Rename the loop variable to make the intent explicit.

`shouldNotifyCodexModelGovernancePending` loads every governance record via `ListCodexModelGovernanceRecords("")` and scans them in memory just to find one model by name. Since the record table has a `uniqueIndex` on `model_name`, a targeted lookup (e.g. `DB.First(&record, "model_name = ?", modelName)` handling `gorm.ErrRecordNotFound`) would avoid the full-table fetch on every finding. Minor, but cleaner and more scalable.

Restore after a 'removed' decision is a silent no-op. When a record is reviewed as `removed`, `removeCodexModelFromChannelsWithDB` strips `modelName` from `Channel.Models` and rebuilds abilities, so the model no longer exists on any channel. A later review setting status back to `active` calls `restoreCodexModelAbilitiesWithDB`, which resolves channels via `filterEnabledCodexChannelIDsWithModel` (matches only channels whose `Models` still contain the model). After removal that returns nothing, so no abilities are re-enabled and the restore succeeds without restoring anything — leaving the model permanently unavailable with no error. Consider either re-adding the model to `Channel.Models` on restore, or rejecting the removed->active transition.
comments.existing_code:
case CodexModelGovernanceStatusActive:
		actionChanged, err := restoreCodexModelAbilitiesWithDB(tx, record.ModelName, channelIDs)

comments.content: The governance record and the ability mutation are not atomic. The record is created/updated and committed first, then `DisableCodexModelAbilities` runs in its own separate transaction. If the disable fails, the function returns `(&record, err)` with a persisted `unsupported_pending_review` record while the abilities remain enabled — the model stays routable despite being flagged, with no rollback. Consider performing the record persistence and the abilities-disable within a single transaction (e.g. via a `*gorm.DB`-aware variant like the other helpers).

TOCTOU race on the unique `model_name`. This does a read (`First`) followed by a separate `Create` on `ErrRecordNotFound`. Two concurrent invocations for the same model (e.g. probe goroutine + notice handler) can both miss the row and both `Create`, triggering a unique-constraint violation and dropping a legitimate detection. The codebase already uses `clause.OnConflict` (see model/ability.go); consider an atomic upsert or retrying the update path on unique violation.

The env-var fallback here is effectively unreachable. Because `AIAnalysisBaseURL` defaults to the non-empty `DefaultMonitorAIAnalysisBaseURL` (line 43), the first check `baseURL != ""` is virtually always true, so `os.Getenv(MonitorAIAnalysisBaseURLEnv)` is never consulted unless a user explicitly sets the config to an empty string. If the intent is for the env var to act as a fallback/override, either set the struct default to empty (`AIAnalysisBaseURL: ""`) so the getter resolves env → constant default, or invert the precedence to check the env var first. The same issue applies to `GetMonitorAIAnalysisModel`.

When governance is disabled, the loop still sleeps for the full `interval` (default `ProbeIntervalMinutes` = 1440, i.e. 24h) before re-evaluating `setting.Enabled`. As a result, if an operator enables governance after startup, the task won't pick it up for up to a day. Consider polling on a short fixed interval while disabled (e.g. sleep 1 minute and `continue`) and only sleeping the configured probe interval after an actual run.

A panic raised inside `runCodexModelGovernanceProbeOnce` / `runCodexOfficialNoticeMonitorOnce` (e.g. from a downstream relay/test/AI call) is recovered by gopool's worker, which unwinds this task function and terminates the loop. Because `codexModelGovernanceTaskOnce` has already fired, the goroutine is never restarted, permanently disabling Codex governance until the process restarts. Wrap the per-iteration work in a `defer/recover` block so a transient panic only aborts a single cycle.

These four constants (and the `codexOfficialNoticeAIBaseURL`/`codexOfficialNoticeAIModel` resolver helpers below) duplicate the canonical definitions in `setting/operation_setting/monitor_setting.go` (`DefaultMonitorAIAnalysisBaseURL`, `DefaultMonitorAIAnalysisModelName`, `MonitorAIAnalysisBaseURLEnv`, `MonitorAIAnalysisModelEnv`, and the `GetMonitorAIAnalysis*` getters). Since the real caller already resolves these via `operation_setting`, maintaining a second source of truth here risks silent drift (e.g. updating the default model in only one place). Consider importing the `operation_setting` constants/getters rather than redefining them.

`affected_channel_ids` is typed as `number[]`, but the backend serializes it from `DecodeCodexModelGovernanceChannelIDs`, which returns a nil slice when a record has no affected channels. A nil Go slice marshals to JSON `null`, so this field can arrive as `null`. The outer `records` array is null-guarded via `?? []`, but inner fields are not, so `channelIds.length` / `channelIds.map(...)` will throw a TypeError and crash the table render. Add a guard.
comments.suggestion_code:
const formatChannelIds = (channelIds: number[] | null | undefined): string => {
  if (!channelIds || channelIds.length === 0) return '-'
comments.existing_code:
const formatChannelIds = (channelIds: number[]): string => {
  if (channelIds.length === 0) return '-'

comments.content: The Textarea prefills from `record.review_note` (`notesById[record.id] ?? record.review_note ?? ''`), but the submitted payload only reads `notesById` and falls back to `''`. If an admin reviews a record without editing the prefilled note, the backend (`review_note: strings.TrimSpace(note)`) overwrites the previously saved note with an empty string, causing silent data loss. Use the same fallback chain here.
